description: Schedule parameters and calendar rules. Load on-demand during planning.

timezone.dst_rule: Last Sunday of March → CEST; Last Sunday of October → CET
timezone.conversion_reminder: ALWAYS convert Outlook UTC → Berlin. Double-check each event lands on the correct day and hour.

calendar_rules.immovable: Accepted and organizer events. Never schedule over them.
calendar_rules.tentative: Show with (tentative) marker. Can schedule tasks in the slot if needed, flagging conflict.
calendar_rules.gtd_category: Calendar events categorized as 'GTD' are previous time-blocks. Treat as available time — do NOT display as meetings.
calendar_rules.buffer: 5-minute gaps between blocks for context switching.

capacity.rule: Fill ~80% of available time. Leave 20% buffer for overflow and context switching.

task_nature_categories.name: follow-ups
task_nature_categories.batching: Group all follow-ups into one block

task_nature_categories.name: compliance
task_nature_categories.batching: Batch all compliance into one slot

task_nature_categories.name: admin
task_nature_categories.batching: End of day or gaps between meetings

scheduling_discipline.step_1_map_calendar: BEFORE placing any tasks, fetch the full Outlook calendar for the target date range. Identify all immovable events (accepted meetings, organizer meetings). Mark available windows.
scheduling_discipline.step_2_compute_windows: Subtract immovable events and lunch from business hours. The result is the set of available windows. Each window has a start time, end time, and duration in minutes.
scheduling_discipline.step_3_place_sequentially: Place tasks one by one into available windows. Each task's startDate must equal or follow the previous task's dueDate. Never overlap tasks with each other or with calendar events.
scheduling_discipline.step_4_respect_buffers: Leave 5-minute gaps between tasks for context switching (per calendar_rules.buffer). Exception: batched follow-ups can be placed back-to-back.
scheduling_discipline.step_5_verify_no_overlaps: After placing all tasks, scan the full schedule for overlaps: task-vs-task and task-vs-calendar-event. If any overlap exists, shift the conflicting task to the next available slot.

deadline_inference.description: Rules for automatically inferring deadline metadata when creating or scheduling tasks. Applied at Step 3.5 (planning) and Mode 3 (task creation). Inference produces a single line: deadline: YYYY-MM-DD [HH:MM] | reason. See deadline-config.json for format details.

patterns.trigger: Task title contains prep-like keywords AND task is tagged with a person name that matches a calendar event attendee
patterns.logic: Look up the next calendar event with the tagged person. Set deadline = event start time. Reason = meeting name.
patterns.example_input: Prep roadmap review (tag: malte) + Outlook shows 'Jourfixe MK <> CG' on Mon 15:00

patterns.trigger: Task tagged with a person name and the next 1-on-1 with that person exists on the calendar
patterns.logic: Resolve the next 1-on-1 meeting with the tagged person from Outlook. Set deadline = meeting start time. Reason = meeting name. This captures bring-up items that should happen before/during the 1:1.
patterns.example_input: Discuss analytics exposé (tag: mo) + Outlook shows 'Mo <> Carlos' on Tue 10:00

patterns.trigger: Task title or context mentions code freeze, release, deployment, or a specific initiative with a known deadline
patterns.logic: Extract the deadline date from context (conversation, Jira initiative, or stated by Carlos). Set deadline = that date. Reason = event name.
patterns.example_input: Sally's BPW tickets before code freeze (context: Apr 20 code freeze)

patterns.trigger: Task involves GDPR, NIS2, DPIA, AVV, DSK, Datenschutz, or other compliance keywords
patterns.logic: Compliance tasks have real legal deadlines. If Carlos states a deadline, use it. If not, ask — do NOT guess. Compliance deadlines should never be inferred silently.
patterns.example_input: DPIA for new push notification feature (Carlos says: needs to be done before next sprint)
patterns.certainty: medium — only if Carlos stated the date

patterns.trigger: Task is a follow-up email, ping, or check-in with no hard external constraint
patterns.logic: Social follow-ups have a soft courtesy window. Default: 2 weekdays from now. Reason = 'follow-up courtesy'. This is the softest deadline — freely movable.
patterns.example_input: Follow up with Mo about analytics

patterns.trigger: Carlos explicitly states a deadline in conversation ('by Friday', 'before Tuesday', 'needs to happen by April 3')
patterns.logic: Parse the explicit date. Convert relative references ('by Friday', 'next week') to absolute dates. Reason = Carlos's stated context.
patterns.example_input: I need Timo's onboarding task created before Tuesday the 7th

patterns.trigger: Task originates from CTO (Malte), CEO, or other executive with implicit urgency
patterns.logic: Executive requests default to 2 weekdays unless explicitly stated otherwise. Reason = requester.
patterns.example_input: Malte wants an update on NIS2 readiness

patterns.trigger: No keywords match, no person tag with upcoming meeting, no explicit date, no context clues
patterns.logic: Do NOT invent a deadline. Leave the task description without a deadline line. Task becomes 'unanchored' — most movable during rebalancing.

inference_rules: Apply patterns in order from most specific to least specific. First match wins.
Pattern priority: explicit_date_stated > meeting_prep > 1on1_discussion_item > code_freeze_release > compliance_regulatory > executive_request > follow_up_courtesy > no_inferable_deadline.
If certainty is 'low' or 'medium', show the inferred deadline in the triage/plan table so Carlos can override before writing to TickTick.
If a task matches multiple patterns, use the one with the earliest deadline (most constraining).
Calendar lookups for meeting anchoring use the Outlook data already fetched in Step 1. No extra API calls.
When resolving person tags to meetings, use the meeting_task_matching rules from this file.
NEVER infer deadlines for recurring tasks.

meeting_task_matching.rule: Tasks tagged with a person name should surface as 💬 bring-up items ONLY during 1-on-1 meetings with that person. Never during large group meetings.
meeting_task_matching.matching: Match task tags (lowercase) against meeting attendee email prefixes (e.g., tag 'malte' → malte.kalkoffen@endios.de). Also match task title keywords against meeting subject.
meeting_task_matching.skipped_1on1: If a 1-on-1 is skipped, use AskUserQuestion to ask about EACH tagged task individually — defer to next week, handle async, or bring up in a different meeting.
